nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical and subtropical seas; rocky substrate, sandy areas, and reef flats in the photic zone

imageCandidates.notes: Wikimedia Commons contributor (author not named in file metadata), 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ reef shot of a discrete, vibrant-green clump with the large, rounded, pronounced bulbous grape-cluster fronds diagnostic of C. racemosa var. macrophysa; clean single-subject framing.
Coughdrop12, 4032x3024. Lush bright-green grape clusters against a blue background (trade/tub look) clearly showing the round bulbous side-shoots; slightly softer/busier than the recommended pick.
Cricket Raspet, 2048x1536. Vibrant macro emphasising the granular 'green dot' texture of the large round bulbs; kept as detail extra (tight crop, some feathery algae intrudes at edges).

summary: Green Bulb Caulerpa is a variety of Sea Grapes with notably larger, rounder bulbous fronds. It grows quickly and is generally considered beginner-accessible, but releases compounds that can stress corals and is a documented invasive species — never release into natural waterways.
careNotes:
Green Bulb Caulerpa (Caulerpa racemosa var. macrophysa) is a variety of Sea Grapes ([sw-algae-016]) distinguished by its notably larger, rounder, more pronounced bulbous side-shoots that give the fronds a characteristic 'green dot' appearance. Growth form is otherwise identical to the parent species: a creeping stolon anchors into substrate or rockwork via small root-like structures called rhizoids, with upright branching axes bearing the familiar grape-cluster fronds. This variety is less commonly stocked than the parent species, and its care follows the same standards established for C. racemosa and the Caulerpa genus broadly.

ALLELOPATHY: As a C. racemosa variety, Green Bulb Caulerpa is documented to release secondary metabolites — including caulerpenyne and caulerpin — into the water column. These compounds have demonstrated inhibitory effects on corals and competing algae in research settings. Physical separation from sensitive SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals), use of activated carbon, and regular harvesting are generally advisable.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: Like all Caulerpa species, var. macrophysa can undergo holocarpy — a mass reproductive event in which the organism's cytoplasm is converted into gametes and released into the tank, potentially causing an acute water-quality crisis. This is typically triggered by sharp nutrient crashes, disrupted photoperiod, or rapid temperature swings. Recommended mitigations include running refugium lighting on a 24-hour photoperiod, harvesting the colony regularly to keep it in active vegetative growth, maintaining stable (non-zero) nutrient levels, and immediately removing any specimen showing reproductive structures (white or translucent fronds, turbid discharge).

INVASIVE CONCERN: C. racemosa and its varieties are documented invasive species, particularly in the Mediterranean (where var. cylindracea has spread extensively). Captive specimens must never be released into natural waterways.

GENERAL CARE: High reef-quality lighting at a spectrum of 5,000K or higher is generally preferred, with a stable photoperiod. Moderate water flow is generally advised. Green Bulb Caulerpa is compatible with snails, hermit crabs, shrimp, and most reef fish, though herbivorous fish — including tangs, rabbitfish, and sea urchins — will readily graze it. Its ornamental grape-cluster frond structure makes it well suited to display placement. Freshly arrived cuttings may arrive with white or translucent fronds from transport darkness; normal coloration typically returns within hours of light exposure.
breedingNotes: Green Bulb Caulerpa propagates readily through fragmentation — broken pieces have been reported to take hold and grow into new plants — and by vegetative tip growth spreading along the stolon.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole aquarium trade source for this variety. Product page confirms common name, scientific name (C. racemosa var. macrophysa), display placement, fast growth, and invasive concern. Husbandry parameters extrapolated from parent species C. racemosa (sw-algae-016) and genus-level Caulerpa care standards.

additional.name: AquaMate — Sea Grapes (Caulerpa racemosa)
additional.notes: Cross-reference: parent species entry (sw-algae-016). Water parameters, lighting, tank minimums, allelopathy, holocarpy, and invasive caveats directly inherited from this entry, which itself was sourced from AlgaeBarn and Wikipedia.

additional.name: AquaMate — Caulerpa prolifera
additional.notes: Sibling species cross-reference (sw-algae-004). Confirms genus-level allelopathy, holocarpy risk, and invasive classification for Caulerpa as a genus.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
